The value of getting a tea in the glass doesn't change, right? But everybody thinks it does just because he's been here for 40 years. So the guy who goes to work every day for 10 years thinks he's entitled, whoever used the word, Linda, to more money than the guy who just got hired this year. The only people who don't think that way basically are salespeople who are on straight commission. Those are the only people who don't think that way. But everybody else, that's their worker head, right? Is there's a link between longevity and tenure and pay. And the longer I show, the more I show up, the more money I should get, even though I am doing exactly the same job that I was doing when I started showing up 10 years ago. Now, businesses, most small business owners, remember, go to their business every morning just like a guy goes to a job. They don't go to it like you and I go. They have a worker head still on their shoulders. So what do they think about this? They think they believe the longer they're at it, automatically, intrinsically, in terms of economic justice, right? A, they should be making more money. And B, they damn sure shouldn't have to deal with any of the stuff we're talking to them about in order to make more money because they have been there longer. So that's in their way. Then we have the real absence of ambition. Now, this is a biggie. It's a biggie because you and I think most of you in this room are pretty ambitious and have been ambitious, if not all your lives, for a long period of time. And so we tend to think that everybody has the same view of ambition that we do. And so we talk to people like they do. But very, very few people have the same view of ambition that we do. They have all sorts of different views, but they don't necessarily have the same view. And many have already, if they had it, they've settled in and they've turned it off. And they're pretty much just now going through the motions. So again, what do most workers do who have jobs and they're in the same job for a period of time? At some point, what does their ambition level? How would you describe their ambition? Where does their ambition arrive? Doing enough, doing enough not to get fired. Right. Doing enough not to get fired. So we're really our small business owners. Doing enough not to go out of business. Doing enough to get to bills paid. That's where they settle into. And so really trying to get them to do much more is sort of annoying. I mean, it's like at this point, you know, like we have that little dog, Carla every once in a while watches Caesar or whatever the hell is, you know, you know what I'm talking about? You know, the guy with all the red dog tracks. And so every once in a while we have to have this conversation that, because our dogs very badly behaved. And from a human perspective, I'm sure the dog thinks we're badly behaved, you know? I mean, but from our perspective. So every once in a while, Carla and I have to have this conversation where Carla says, hey, we should, you know, take the dog to an obedience trainer person. You know, I saw these DVD, which the DVD things say, got to order Caesar's dog training DVDs. See, this is like Barbara Streisand putting up a clothesline. You know, it's another job for James Brolin, right? So the dog training thing, if you don't, well, never mind. The joke used to work better about five years ago when everybody knew who the hell James Brolin was. So the DVDs would be another job for Dan, you know, and I already got a couple of jobs. So I discourage that on that basis. But I have to explain to her fundamentally it's too late. I mean, all you're going to do now at this point is just annoy the hell out of the dog, right? The dog has settled in. I mean, it has decided the way things are going to be and it has really no ambition to please us any more than it already does, right? Because it's getting everything it wants. So why exactly, you know, and so most business owners really have sort of settled into this place where regardless of what they say, their ambition is actually dormant, right? It's turned way down to the equivalent of doing enough not to get fired. And there's a profound difference between desire and ambition. See, everybody has desire for more. Almost everybody. Maybe we can find some who don't. But very few people will turn it down. You know, they play the lottery every week. They put money in a slot machine. They they're nice to Aunt Mabel as she heads up to point of death in hope that she's going to leave something to him. Nobody sits down with Aunt Mabel and says, I'm going to be nice to you anyway, but feel free to give it all to charity. Nobody has that conversation. I haven't had that conversation with anybody in our family. Nobody has come to me and said, you know.
